evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | At the left-rear coolant-plate outlet on Aurex BatteryHub-612 LR, record the complaint as seepage, dried residue, or undertray drip, and capture the coolant concentration, pack serial, and the last chiller or hose service that touched this branch. Save the as-found stain path from the outlet neck to the shield edge before any clip, hose, or connector body is moved. |
| T2 | Make the pack safe, remove the local underbody shield, and expose the aluminum outlet neck, PA12 quick-connector shell, green EPDM O-ring seat, stainless retainer clip, hose saddle bracket, and nearby harness fir-tree clip without changing the hose angle. Keep the connector-to-bracket path intact so the original side load can still be judged. |
| T3 | Clean only the loose dirt and then trace the residue from the lowest drip point upward with a lamp, separating wetting at the latch window from wetting at the tube bead or the cold-plate seam. Residue that begins above the retainer clip and then runs down the shell suggests a different boundary than residue that forms first at the aluminum neck. |
| T4 | Cap the neighboring branch and apply a controlled pressure hold to the suspect section, then watch the connector shell, O-ring land, and plate seam for the first fresh bead. Record the applied pressure and the time to first wetting so the leak proof is tied to one repeatable condition. |
| T5 | Compare insertion depth, retainer-clip seating, hose twist, and bracket position against the right-rear outlet on the same pack because a coupler held 3 mm short of full engagement can mimic a seam leak. Use the opposite-side outlet as the geometry reference before deciding which part is actually failed. |
| T6 | Correct only the identified interface by renewing the O-ring, replacing the cracked connector shell, deburring the outlet bead, or resetting the saddle bracket so the hose leaves the neck without upward pull. Confirm that the retainer clip locks fully into both grooves and that the bracket no longer pushes the shell off axis. |
| T7 | Circulate coolant through the restored branch and compare inlet and outlet temperatures plus the local shell condition with the pre-repair state while the branch remains visible. The proof is incomplete if the connector stays dry at static pressure but begins to sweat once flow and vibration return. |
| T8 | Release this branch only when the note names the exact failed boundary, such as O-ring seat, connector shell, retainer clip engagement, outlet bead, or saddle bracket side load, and includes the pressure and circulation results taken after repair. The accepted condition is a dry left-rear outlet path with stable hose alignment and no renewed staining from neck to shield edge. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: At the left-rear coolant-plate outlet on Aurex BatteryHub-612 LR, record the complaint as seepage, dried residue, or undertray drip, and capture the coolant concentration, pack serial, and the last chiller or hose service that touched this branch. Save the as-found stain path from the outlet neck to the shield edge before any clip, hose, or connector body is moved.

concept_mentions.label: as-found stain path
concept_mentions.description: stain path observed before moving parts

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Make the pack safe, remove the local underbody shield, and expose the aluminum outlet neck, PA12 quick-connector shell, green EPDM O-ring seat, stainless retainer clip, hose saddle bracket, and nearby harness fir-tree clip without changing the hose angle. Keep the connector-to-bracket path intact so the original side load can still be judged.

concept_mentions.label: stainless retainer clip
concept_mentions.description: retainer clip made of stainless steel

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Clean only the loose dirt and then trace the residue from the lowest drip point upward with a lamp, separating wetting at the latch window from wetting at the tube bead or the cold-plate seam. Residue that begins above the retainer clip and then runs down the shell suggests a different boundary than residue that forms first at the aluminum neck.

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Cap the neighboring branch and apply a controlled pressure hold to the suspect section, then watch the connector shell, O-ring land, and plate seam for the first fresh bead. Record the applied pressure and the time to first wetting so the leak proof is tied to one repeatable condition.

concept_mentions.label: first fresh bead
concept_mentions.description: first bead of wetting under pressure

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Compare insertion depth, retainer-clip seating, hose twist, and bracket position against the right-rear outlet on the same pack because a coupler held 3 mm short of full engagement can mimic a seam leak. Use the opposite-side outlet as the geometry reference before deciding which part is actually failed.

concept_mentions.label: coupler held 3 mm short of full engagement
concept_mentions.description: fault where coupler is not fully engaged
concept_mentions.surface_form: coupler held 3 mm short of full engagement

concept_mentions.label: insertion depth
concept_mentions.description: depth of insertion for the connector

concept_mentions.label: retainer-clip seating
concept_mentions.description: seating condition of the retainer clip

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Correct only the identified interface by renewing the O-ring, replacing the cracked connector shell, deburring the outlet bead, or resetting the saddle bracket so the hose leaves the neck without upward pull. Confirm that the retainer clip locks fully into both grooves and that the bracket no longer pushes the shell off axis.

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: Circulate coolant through the restored branch and compare inlet and outlet temperatures plus the local shell condition with the pre-repair state while the branch remains visible. The proof is incomplete if the connector stays dry at static pressure but begins to sweat once flow and vibration return.

task.label: T8
task.surface_form: Release this branch only when the note names the exact failed boundary, such as O-ring seat, connector shell, retainer clip engagement, outlet bead, or saddle bracket side load, and includes the pressure and circulation results taken after repair. The accepted condition is a dry left-rear outlet path with stable hose alignment and no renewed staining from neck to shield edge.

concept_mentions.label: dry left-rear outlet path
concept_mentions.description: dry path at the left-rear outlet

concept_mentions.label: pressure and circulation results
concept_mentions.description: results from pressure and circulation tests

concept_mentions.label: saddle bracket side load
concept_mentions.description: side load on the saddle bracket

document_relation_mentions.label: indicates
document_relation_mentions.head: coupler held 3 mm short of full engagement

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | For Helion PackCore-544 AWD, capture whether the issue at the front manifold face is coolant odor, slow level loss, or wetness after recent manifold removal, and note the service history that may have altered bolt clamp load or seal compression. Record the manifold-side residue pattern before the first bolt is touched. |
| T2 | Safe the pack and remove the front manifold cover so the composite manifold body, M6 retaining bolts, steel inserts, upper and lower hose necks, perimeter O-ring channel, and nearby NTC loom can be seen together. The manifold and its adjacent hose loads need to be read in one view because a distorted hose path can change flange compression. |
| T3 | Inspect bolt witness paint, washer seating, flange gap, and dried coolant crystals around the full perimeter, then compare the upper-left and lower-right corners where clamp load often diverges first. A local flange opening at one boss points to a different repair boundary than even residue around the entire channel. |
| T4 | Lift the manifold only enough to inspect the EPDM O-ring cross-section and the groove corners at the inlet crossover, checking for twist, flattening, nicked lips, or dry installation marks. Keep dirt out of the aluminum seal land so the later compression evidence remains reliable. |
| T5 | Lay the manifold back against the face and measure flange stand-off at several bolts while watching whether one hose neck or bracket pushes the body off the header plate. If the top hose must be forced downward to start the bolt, the boundary is likely hose-induced preload or manifold warp rather than the seal alone. |
| T6 | Inspect the plastic body for bow, the steel inserts for pull-up, and the bolt stack for extra washer thickness or uneven bolt stretch so clamp loss is assigned to a real mechanical cause. This prevents the same leak from being mislabeled as a generic O-ring failure. |
| T7 | Replace or reseat only the failed manifold, O-ring, bolt stack, or hose support item, then torque the manifold in sequence from the center outward and confirm that the flange gap closes evenly at all corners. The hose necks should sit naturally with no side pull on the composite body. |
| T8 | Pressure-test and circulate the restored manifold branch while monitoring the manifold face, hose spigots, and the nearby NTC temperature trend because a trapped air pocket or warped flange can reopen only after the plastic body warms. Record both the dry condition and the thermal behavior during this proof cycle. |
| T9 | Mark the manifold repair complete only when the record identifies the exact failure mode, such as twisted perimeter O-ring, warped composite flange, insert pull-up, overlength bolt stack, or hose-side preload, and ties it to the repeated pressure and circulation result. The release boundary is a manifold face that stays dry at all bosses with even clamp witness and stable temperature response across the repaired front branch. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: For Helion PackCore-544 AWD, capture whether the issue at the front manifold face is coolant odor, slow level loss, or wetness after recent manifold removal, and note the service history that may have altered bolt clamp load or seal compression. Record the manifold-side residue pattern before the first bolt is touched.

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Safe the pack and remove the front manifold cover so the composite manifold body, M6 retaining bolts, steel inserts, upper and lower hose necks, perimeter O-ring channel, and nearby NTC loom can be seen together. The manifold and its adjacent hose loads need to be read in one view because a distorted hose path can change flange compression.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Inspect bolt witness paint, washer seating, flange gap, and dried coolant crystals around the full perimeter, then compare the upper-left and lower-right corners where clamp load often diverges first. A local flange opening at one boss points to a different repair boundary than even residue around the entire channel.

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Lift the manifold only enough to inspect the EPDM O-ring cross-section and the groove corners at the inlet crossover, checking for twist, flattening, nicked lips, or dry installation marks. Keep dirt out of the aluminum seal land so the later compression evidence remains reliable.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Lay the manifold back against the face and measure flange stand-off at several bolts while watching whether one hose neck or bracket pushes the body off the header plate. If the top hose must be forced downward to start the bolt, the boundary is likely hose-induced preload or manifold warp rather than the seal alone.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Inspect the plastic body for bow, the steel inserts for pull-up, and the bolt stack for extra washer thickness or uneven bolt stretch so clamp loss is assigned to a real mechanical cause. This prevents the same leak from being mislabeled as a generic O-ring failure.

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: Replace or reseat only the failed manifold, O-ring, bolt stack, or hose support item, then torque the manifold in sequence from the center outward and confirm that the flange gap closes evenly at all corners. The hose necks should sit naturally with no side pull on the composite body.

task.label: T8
task.surface_form: Pressure-test and circulate the restored manifold branch while monitoring the manifold face, hose spigots, and the nearby NTC temperature trend because a trapped air pocket or warped flange can reopen only after the plastic body warms. Record both the dry condition and the thermal behavior during this proof cycle.

task.label: T9
task.surface_form: Mark the manifold repair complete only when the record identifies the exact failure mode, such as twisted perimeter O-ring, warped composite flange, insert pull-up, overlength bolt stack, or hose-side preload, and ties it to the repeated pressure and circulation result. The release boundary is a manifold face that stays dry at all bosses with even clamp witness and stable temperature response across the repaired front branch.

concept_mentions.label: pressure and circulation result
concept_mentions.description: result of pressure test and circulation

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | Before replacing the chiller-inlet connector on Novaris ThermalPack-481, document the original failure as cracked shell, broken latch ear, incomplete insertion, or recurring seepage at the inlet tube bead, and note the exact operating state that exposed it. Keep the pack-to-chiller hose and the adjacent support clip undisturbed until the as-found angle is recorded. |
| T2 | Isolate the cooling branch, recover residual coolant, and expose the tube neck, quick-connector shell, internal retainer, green O-ring, hose overmold, support clip, and nearby case rib while preserving the original hose clocking. Photograph the connector-to-clip path so the load direction remains traceable after removal. |
| T3 | Release the connector with the approved tool and inspect the removed shell for stress whitening, latch-window distortion, inner-retainer wear, and witness marks that show whether the shell rotated or walked under vibration. A shell that is polished on one side only usually indicates bracket side load rather than poor seal material. |
| T4 | Inspect the aluminum tube bead, lead-in chamfer, and O-ring land for burrs, nicks, or corrosion that could cut the fresh seal during installation. Measure the bead condition and insertion depth reference before fitting the new hardware so the new connector is not asked to mask a damaged neck. |
| T5 | Compare the replacement connector, seal, and retainer with the removed parts, then lubricate the new O-ring as specified and install the shell square to the tube neck. Confirm that the retainer windows show full engagement and that the hose overmold is not twisted relative to the case rib. |
| T6 | Refit the support clip and verify that the hose leaves the connector with the intended bend radius, upper-to-lower offset, and clearance to the pack case. If the clip position shortens the hose path and starts to tilt the shell, correct the support geometry before any pressure test begins. |
| T7 | Apply a static pressure hold and watch the new shell, O-ring interface, and tube bead for fresh wetting, then compare the result with the as-found leak path. Static proof is useful only if the connector remains centered while pressure rises. |
| T8 | Run coolant through the repaired inlet branch and observe the connector while the pump cycles and the branch warms, because some replacement connectors stay dry at rest but sweat once the hose relaxes into its loaded position. Record both flow condition and surface state during this dynamic check. |
| T9 | Reinstall the access panels and repeat the same operating state that originally produced the leak so the final judgment reflects the guarded configuration and the real support path from connector to clip. The restored branch has to survive shield preload as well as coolant flow. |
| T10 | Close the work only after the note names the replaced and inspected items at component level, such as quick-connector shell, O-ring, inner retainer, tube bead, or support clip, and records the static and dynamic proof results beside them. The accepted state is a chiller-inlet joint that remains dry, fully latched, and naturally supported from tube neck to first clip. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: Before replacing the chiller-inlet connector on Novaris ThermalPack-481, document the original failure as cracked shell, broken latch ear, incomplete insertion, or recurring seepage at the inlet tube bead, and note the exact operating state that exposed it. Keep the pack-to-chiller hose and the adjacent support clip undisturbed until the as-found angle is recorded.

concept_mentions.label: support clip
concept_mentions.description: support clip for hose or connector

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Isolate the cooling branch, recover residual coolant, and expose the tube neck, quick-connector shell, internal retainer, green O-ring, hose overmold, support clip, and nearby case rib while preserving the original hose clocking. Photograph the connector-to-clip path so the load direction remains traceable after removal.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Release the connector with the approved tool and inspect the removed shell for stress whitening, latch-window distortion, inner-retainer wear, and witness marks that show whether the shell rotated or walked under vibration. A shell that is polished on one side only usually indicates bracket side load rather than poor seal material.

concept_mentions.label: shell rotated or walked under vibration
concept_mentions.description: shell rotation or walking under vibration
concept_mentions.surface_form: shell rotated or walked under vibration

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Inspect the aluminum tube bead, lead-in chamfer, and O-ring land for burrs, nicks, or corrosion that could cut the fresh seal during installation. Measure the bead condition and insertion depth reference before fitting the new hardware so the new connector is not asked to mask a damaged neck.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Compare the replacement connector, seal, and retainer with the removed parts, then lubricate the new O-ring as specified and install the shell square to the tube neck. Confirm that the retainer windows show full engagement and that the hose overmold is not twisted relative to the case rib.

concept_mentions.label: hose overmold not twisted
concept_mentions.description: state of hose overmold not being twisted

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Refit the support clip and verify that the hose leaves the connector with the intended bend radius, upper-to-lower offset, and clearance to the pack case. If the clip position shortens the hose path and starts to tilt the shell, correct the support geometry before any pressure test begins.

concept_mentions.label: support clip
concept_mentions.description: support clip for hose or connector

concept_mentions.label: upper-to-lower offset
concept_mentions.description: offset between upper and lower parts

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: Apply a static pressure hold and watch the new shell, O-ring interface, and tube bead for fresh wetting, then compare the result with the as-found leak path. Static proof is useful only if the connector remains centered while pressure rises.

task.label: T8
task.surface_form: Run coolant through the repaired inlet branch and observe the connector while the pump cycles and the branch warms, because some replacement connectors stay dry at rest but sweat once the hose relaxes into its loaded position. Record both flow condition and surface state during this dynamic check.

task.label: T9
task.surface_form: Reinstall the access panels and repeat the same operating state that originally produced the leak so the final judgment reflects the guarded configuration and the real support path from connector to clip. The restored branch has to survive shield preload as well as coolant flow.

concept_mentions.label: real support path from connector to clip
concept_mentions.surface_form: real support path from connector to clip

task.label: T10
task.surface_form: Close the work only after the note names the replaced and inspected items at component level, such as quick-connector shell, O-ring, inner retainer, tube bead, or support clip, and records the static and dynamic proof results beside them. The accepted state is a chiller-inlet joint that remains dry, fully latched, and naturally supported from tube neck to first clip.

concept_mentions.label: static and dynamic proof results
concept_mentions.description: results from static and dynamic tests

document_concept_mentions.label: shell polished on one side
document_concept_mentions.description: shell polished on one side only

document_relation_mentions.label: indicates
document_relation_mentions.head: witness marks
document_relation_mentions.tail: shell rotated or walked under vibration

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | On Kestrel VMC-850, record the spindle chiller complaint as coolant dripping from the rear column only during circulation, residue collecting on the lower cable tray, or coolant loss that appears after a sustained 8000 to 12000 rpm spindle hold. Note the as-received coolant level, spindle speed window, and the first visible drip path before any guard is removed. |
| T2 | Isolate the machine as required and expose the spindle-chiller supply hose, return hose, worm-drive clamps, brass tube necks, chiller manifold block, P-clip on the rear column, and the lower drip tray. Keep the upper outlet hose and lower return hose visible together so upstream and downstream wetting can be separated instead of being read as one large stain. |
| T3 | Trace the wet boundary from the lowest drip point back toward the upper hose bead, the clamp screw housing, the molded hose bend, or the manifold face, and document whether the stain runs front to rear along the cable tray or drops straight down from the hose neck. Preserve hose twist and clip position because a side-loaded branch often leaks only when the machine is in its final supported geometry. |
| T4 | Pressure-test the branch at 1.5 bar with the spindle stopped and compare the result with the dry reference hose on the opposite side of the chiller block. If the branch stays dry under static pressure but wets the lower tray once the chiller pump runs, keep the evidence on vibration load, clamp position, and hose insertion depth rather than replacing the manifold on assumption. |
| T5 | Measure clamp position relative to the tube bead and check hose insertion depth at both the upper outlet neck and the lower return neck. A clamp that sits more than a few millimeters behind the bead or a hose that is pulled rearward by the P-clip can look seated while still opening a leak path once pump pulsation and spindle vibration act on the branch together. |
| T6 | Run the chiller pump and hold the spindle at the complaint speed with the rear guard still open, then watch the upper clamp, the molded bend, and the P-clip bracket through the full vibration event. If wetting starts only when the hose is tugged toward the rear column clip, isolate the fault boundary to the upper return-hose clamp and clip-to-bracket side load rather than to the manifold casting or the lower drain path. |
| T7 | Reposition the hose fully against the tube bead, rotate the clamp so the screw housing does not contact the column bracket, and restore the branch through the original clip path with a relaxed bend radius. Confirm that the hose is seated against the bead, the clamp band is centered, and the P-clip is holding the branch without twisting the upper neck sideways. |
| T8 | Release the machine only after a repeated pressure hold and a repeated high-speed circulation run leave the upper hose neck, lower return path, and drip tray dry. The accepted closeout is a named leak boundary at the spindle-chiller return hose clamp or its support path, with no renewed coolant trace from upper outlet to lower tray under the same speed window that created the complaint. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: On Kestrel VMC-850, record the spindle chiller complaint as coolant dripping from the rear column only during circulation, residue collecting on the lower cable tray, or coolant loss that appears after a sustained 8000 to 12000 rpm spindle hold. Note the as-received coolant level, spindle speed window, and the first visible drip path before any guard is removed.

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Isolate the machine as required and expose the spindle-chiller supply hose, return hose, worm-drive clamps, brass tube necks, chiller manifold block, P-clip on the rear column, and the lower drip tray. Keep the upper outlet hose and lower return hose visible together so upstream and downstream wetting can be separated instead of being read as one large stain.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Trace the wet boundary from the lowest drip point back toward the upper hose bead, the clamp screw housing, the molded hose bend, or the manifold face, and document whether the stain runs front to rear along the cable tray or drops straight down from the hose neck. Preserve hose twist and clip position because a side-loaded branch often leaks only when the machine is in its final supported geometry.

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Pressure-test the branch at 1.5 bar with the spindle stopped and compare the result with the dry reference hose on the opposite side of the chiller block. If the branch stays dry under static pressure but wets the lower tray once the chiller pump runs, keep the evidence on vibration load, clamp position, and hose insertion depth rather than replacing the manifold on assumption.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Measure clamp position relative to the tube bead and check hose insertion depth at both the upper outlet neck and the lower return neck. A clamp that sits more than a few millimeters behind the bead or a hose that is pulled rearward by the P-clip can look seated while still opening a leak path once pump pulsation and spindle vibration act on the branch together.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Run the chiller pump and hold the spindle at the complaint speed with the rear guard still open, then watch the upper clamp, the molded bend, and the P-clip bracket through the full vibration event. If wetting starts only when the hose is tugged toward the rear column clip, isolate the fault boundary to the upper return-hose clamp and clip-to-bracket side load rather than to the manifold casting or the lower drain path.

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: Reposition the hose fully against the tube bead, rotate the clamp so the screw housing does not contact the column bracket, and restore the branch through the original clip path with a relaxed bend radius. Confirm that the hose is seated against the bead, the clamp band is centered, and the P-clip is holding the branch without twisting the upper neck sideways.

task.label: T8
task.surface_form: Release the machine only after a repeated pressure hold and a repeated high-speed circulation run leave the upper hose neck, lower return path, and drip tray dry. The accepted closeout is a named leak boundary at the spindle-chiller return hose clamp or its support path, with no renewed coolant trace from upper outlet to lower tray under the same speed window that created the complaint.

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | On Arcline VTC-630, record the complaint as tool pull-out during face milling, weak unclamp feel, or drawbar alarm on a CAT40 spindle, and note the toolholder style, cutting load, and whether the issue followed recent drawbar service. The intake note must already tie the problem to the drawbar clamp stack and not to a generic spindle fault. |
| T2 | Warm the spindle to the service temperature if required, then expose the drawbar booster cylinder, Belleville stack housing, unclamp valve, pressure transducer port, and the access path to the spindle pull-force test point. Keep the upper actuator side and lower retention side visible in the same inspection window so stroke and clamp force can be judged against the same hardware state. |
| T3 | Install the calibrated clamp-force gauge in a verified CAT40 holder, run the clamp cycle, and record the measured pull force rather than relying on sound or feel. Compare the cold and warmed values if the complaint changes with temperature, because a weak Belleville stack and a short hydraulic stroke do not deteriorate in the same way. |
| T4 | Measure unclamp pressure at the booster cylinder and compare it with the machine target while watching drawbar movement. If the supply pressure is healthy but clamp force remains low, the next split should stay inside the mechanical stack, whereas low pressure with normal stack height pushes the boundary toward the hydraulic side of the circuit. |
| T5 | Inspect the Belleville stack height, washer orientation, release-piston clearance, and actuator linkage from upper cylinder to lower drawbar seat. Pay attention to whether one washer pair is inverted or whether the stack height is short at the lower end, because a single reversed pair can reduce clamp force without leaving obvious damage on the piston or pressure circuit. |
| T6 | Compare measured stack height and washer sequence with the service layout, then isolate the fault boundary only after pressure, stroke, and stack geometry are read together. If pressure reaches the target and release stroke is normal while pull force stays low, keep the fault on the Belleville stack orientation or drawbar hardware instead of blaming the valve or pressure supply. |
| T7 | Rebuild the stack in the correct order, restore the actuator linkage, and repeat the pull-force test with the same toolholder and the same spindle temperature window. Confirm that the stack sits against the lower seat, the linkage travels freely, and the pull-force gauge now follows the corrected washer orientation rather than random warm-up variation. |
| T8 | Run three clamp and unclamp cycles while watching pressure recovery, release stroke, and holder retention, then inspect the actuator and cylinder for fresh oil trace or misalignment. The machine is not released on one good force reading if the drawbar still hangs up on the second or third cycle. |
| T9 | Close the work only after the report names the exact failed boundary, such as an inverted lower Belleville pair, reduced stack height, hydraulic short-stroke, or actuator-linkage misalignment, and ties it to the measured force and pressure results. Accept the repair when clamp force, release stroke, and repeated tool retention all remain stable on the corrected drawbar stack. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: On Arcline VTC-630, record the complaint as tool pull-out during face milling, weak unclamp feel, or drawbar alarm on a CAT40 spindle, and note the toolholder style, cutting load, and whether the issue followed recent drawbar service. The intake note must already tie the problem to the drawbar clamp stack and not to a generic spindle fault.

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Warm the spindle to the service temperature if required, then expose the drawbar booster cylinder, Belleville stack housing, unclamp valve, pressure transducer port, and the access path to the spindle pull-force test point. Keep the upper actuator side and lower retention side visible in the same inspection window so stroke and clamp force can be judged against the same hardware state.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Install the calibrated clamp-force gauge in a verified CAT40 holder, run the clamp cycle, and record the measured pull force rather than relying on sound or feel. Compare the cold and warmed values if the complaint changes with temperature, because a weak Belleville stack and a short hydraulic stroke do not deteriorate in the same way.

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Measure unclamp pressure at the booster cylinder and compare it with the machine target while watching drawbar movement. If the supply pressure is healthy but clamp force remains low, the next split should stay inside the mechanical stack, whereas low pressure with normal stack height pushes the boundary toward the hydraulic side of the circuit.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Inspect the Belleville stack height, washer orientation, release-piston clearance, and actuator linkage from upper cylinder to lower drawbar seat. Pay attention to whether one washer pair is inverted or whether the stack height is short at the lower end, because a single reversed pair can reduce clamp force without leaving obvious damage on the piston or pressure circuit.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Compare measured stack height and washer sequence with the service layout, then isolate the fault boundary only after pressure, stroke, and stack geometry are read together. If pressure reaches the target and release stroke is normal while pull force stays low, keep the fault on the Belleville stack orientation or drawbar hardware instead of blaming the valve or pressure supply.

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: Rebuild the stack in the correct order, restore the actuator linkage, and repeat the pull-force test with the same toolholder and the same spindle temperature window. Confirm that the stack sits against the lower seat, the linkage travels freely, and the pull-force gauge now follows the corrected washer orientation rather than random warm-up variation.

task.label: T8
task.surface_form: Run three clamp and unclamp cycles while watching pressure recovery, release stroke, and holder retention, then inspect the actuator and cylinder for fresh oil trace or misalignment. The machine is not released on one good force reading if the drawbar still hangs up on the second or third cycle.

task.label: T9
task.surface_form: Close the work only after the report names the exact failed boundary, such as an inverted lower Belleville pair, reduced stack height, hydraulic short-stroke, or actuator-linkage misalignment, and ties it to the measured force and pressure results. Accept the repair when clamp force, release stroke, and repeated tool retention all remain stable on the corrected drawbar stack.

concept_mentions.label: measured force and pressure results
concept_mentions.description: results of force and pressure measurements

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | On Helion HMC-500, record the complaint as chatter after roughing cast iron, toolholder sticking at the spindle nose, or dark contact marks found during tool change, and note the last operation, coolant condition, and affected holder. This review is focused on the BT40 taper mouth, drive dogs, and air-blast path rather than on the spindle cartridge generally. |
| T2 | Lock out spindle rotation and expose the taper mouth, the deeper taper zone, the drive dogs, the retention interface, the air-blast nozzle, and the lower drain groove without cleaning away the contamination pattern. Keep the upper nozzle and lower mouth visible together so chip entry and coolant pooling can be separated by direction. |
| T3 | Inspect the taper for chip pack, rust bloom, fretting dust, dried coolant, and holder witness at the 12, 3, 6, and 9 o'clock positions, then note whether the heaviest contamination sits at the lower mouth or deeper in the taper. A lower-mouth chip ring points to seating contamination, while deep taper staining pushes the boundary toward internal retention or purge failure. |
| T4 | Measure air-blast pressure at the nozzle and measure radial runout on a clean master arbor at the prescribed gauge line before any corrective cleaning is accepted. Normal runout with low purge pressure indicates a contamination path that starts at the nozzle or drain groove, while poor runout after cleaning pushes the investigation away from debris and toward taper or retention damage. |
| T5 | Compare mouth contact pattern with deeper taper contact after approved cleaning, then inspect the nozzle outlet and drain groove for packed fines that would route slurry downward into the seating area. If the runout stays normal and the contact pattern recovers once the lower mouth and nozzle are cleaned, isolate the fault boundary to chip-packed taper contamination and blocked purge path instead of to the spindle bearings. |
| T6 | Release the spindle only after the record names the contaminant type, the exact affected zone from nozzle to taper mouth to drive-dog area, and the measured recovery in purge pressure and master-arbor runout. The accepted condition is a clean taper that seats a verified holder without sticking and without renewed debris concentration at the lower mouth after the same coolant and tool-change cycle. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: On Helion HMC-500, record the complaint as chatter after roughing cast iron, toolholder sticking at the spindle nose, or dark contact marks found during tool change, and note the last operation, coolant condition, and affected holder. This review is focused on the BT40 taper mouth, drive dogs, and air-blast path rather than on the spindle cartridge generally.

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Lock out spindle rotation and expose the taper mouth, the deeper taper zone, the drive dogs, the retention interface, the air-blast nozzle, and the lower drain groove without cleaning away the contamination pattern. Keep the upper nozzle and lower mouth visible together so chip entry and coolant pooling can be separated by direction.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Inspect the taper for chip pack, rust bloom, fretting dust, dried coolant, and holder witness at the 12, 3, 6, and 9 o'clock positions, then note whether the heaviest contamination sits at the lower mouth or deeper in the taper. A lower-mouth chip ring points to seating contamination, while deep taper staining pushes the boundary toward internal retention or purge failure.

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Measure air-blast pressure at the nozzle and measure radial runout on a clean master arbor at the prescribed gauge line before any corrective cleaning is accepted. Normal runout with low purge pressure indicates a contamination path that starts at the nozzle or drain groove, while poor runout after cleaning pushes the investigation away from debris and toward taper or retention damage.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Compare mouth contact pattern with deeper taper contact after approved cleaning, then inspect the nozzle outlet and drain groove for packed fines that would route slurry downward into the seating area. If the runout stays normal and the contact pattern recovers once the lower mouth and nozzle are cleaned, isolate the fault boundary to chip-packed taper contamination and blocked purge path instead of to the spindle bearings.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Release the spindle only after the record names the contaminant type, the exact affected zone from nozzle to taper mouth to drive-dog area, and the measured recovery in purge pressure and master-arbor runout. The accepted condition is a clean taper that seats a verified holder without sticking and without renewed debris concentration at the lower mouth after the same coolant and tool-change cycle.

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | Begin the leak map on Aurex E-Motion-412 LR by recording battery coolant level, residue color, undertray wetness, recent thermal-system repair history, and whether seepage is already visible at the left coolant-plate outlet while the pack remains in the as-received condition with no covers moved. |
| T2 | Power the vehicle down, isolate the high-voltage pack, and allow the local cooling branch to equalize. Then remove the rear underbody shield and the left pack-side splash cover while keeping the hose angle unchanged so the original quick-coupler seating witness can still be read. |
| T3 | Wipe only the loose surface film first, leave the wet edge untouched, and trace the leak path from the coolant-plate seam toward the outlet hose, the quick-coupler lock window, the O-ring seat, and the nearby bracket drain edge so the actual origin is not blurred by cleaning too early. |
| T4 | Install the local pressure tool on the coolant-plate inlet and outlet, cap the neighboring branch, and hold the specified pressure long enough to observe whether fresh wetting begins at the plate seam, the coupler body, the hose clamp line, or the tube insertion stop inside the connector. |
| T5 | Compare the suspect joint with the opposite coolant-plate branch, paying attention to hose twist, clamp clocking, connector latch height, tube insertion depth, and any side-load mark where the support bracket may have forced the plastic coupler body off-axis. This comparison should distinguish a deformed connector body from a hose-routing load or a clamp-position error before the joint is opened. |
| T6 | Inspect the aluminum plate edge, the molded quick connector, the green O-ring land, the hose end, and the nearby harness clip for dried coolant crystals, abrasion, plastic stress whitening, or metal staining that would separate connector leakage from a plate-side crack. |
| T7 | Mark the confirmed leak boundary directly at the leaking hardware, photograph the coolant-plate seam and the quick-coupler seat from more than one angle, and note clearly whether the failure belongs to the plate seam, the O-ring interface, the connector shell, or the hose-routing condition. |
| T8 | Refit only the access parts needed to protect the opened area, leave the identified leak point readable for downstream repair, and close the record with the exact hardware named as battery coolant-plate outlet quick connector or coolant-plate seam instead of leaving a generic leak description. The accepted leak map should still let the next repair start from one named outlet-side boundary rather than from a broad pack-coolant leak note. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: Begin the leak map on Aurex E-Motion-412 LR by recording battery coolant level, residue color, undertray wetness, recent thermal-system repair history, and whether seepage is already visible at the left coolant-plate outlet while the pack remains in the as-received condition with no covers moved.

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Power the vehicle down, isolate the high-voltage pack, and allow the local cooling branch to equalize. Then remove the rear underbody shield and the left pack-side splash cover while keeping the hose angle unchanged so the original quick-coupler seating witness can still be read.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Wipe only the loose surface film first, leave the wet edge untouched, and trace the leak path from the coolant-plate seam toward the outlet hose, the quick-coupler lock window, the O-ring seat, and the nearby bracket drain edge so the actual origin is not blurred by cleaning too early.

concept_mentions.label: quick-coupler lock window
concept_mentions.description: lock window on quick coupler component

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Install the local pressure tool on the coolant-plate inlet and outlet, cap the neighboring branch, and hold the specified pressure long enough to observe whether fresh wetting begins at the plate seam, the coupler body, the hose clamp line, or the tube insertion stop inside the connector.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Compare the suspect joint with the opposite coolant-plate branch, paying attention to hose twist, clamp clocking, connector latch height, tube insertion depth, and any side-load mark where the support bracket may have forced the plastic coupler body off-axis. This comparison should distinguish a deformed connector body from a hose-routing load or a clamp-position error before the joint is opened.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Inspect the aluminum plate edge, the molded quick connector, the green O-ring land, the hose end, and the nearby harness clip for dried coolant crystals, abrasion, plastic stress whitening, or metal staining that would separate connector leakage from a plate-side crack.

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: Mark the confirmed leak boundary directly at the leaking hardware, photograph the coolant-plate seam and the quick-coupler seat from more than one angle, and note clearly whether the failure belongs to the plate seam, the O-ring interface, the connector shell, or the hose-routing condition.

task.label: T8
task.surface_form: Refit only the access parts needed to protect the opened area, leave the identified leak point readable for downstream repair, and close the record with the exact hardware named as battery coolant-plate outlet quick connector or coolant-plate seam instead of leaving a generic leak description. The accepted leak map should still let the next repair start from one named outlet-side boundary rather than from a broad pack-coolant leak note.

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | At Velorian PackLine-287 AWD, reproduce the service-disconnect seating complaint by noting latch feel, insertion force, handle flushness, any HVIL warning, and whether the orange handle sits proud of the battery access opening or snaps back after seating. Keep the opening complaint tied to the actual handle position and HVIL response so later seat checks stay on the same disconnect boundary. |
| T2 | Depower the pack, verify zero residual high-voltage potential at the service-disconnect cavity, and remove the access lid so the disconnect plug, blade set, guide rail, interlock pins, latch window, and surrounding sealing edge are all visible in one service view. |
| T3 | Pull the service disconnect straight out without rotating it, then inspect the male blades, female socket faces, guide ribs, CPA feature, handle pivot, and latch hook for burrs, arc marks, plastic rollover, uneven insertion witness lines, or debris in the guide channel. |
| T4 | Compare the suspect disconnect with a known-good reference or spare, checking seating depth, latch travel, blade exposure, guide-rib alignment, and the shoulder position of the interlock pins so the seat issue is tied to geometry rather than to feel alone. |
| T5 | Rework the seat condition by correcting the misaligned guide feature, removing only the minor burr or displaced plastic that blocks full travel, and cleaning the contact area without changing the original blade profile, terminal tension, or latch geometry. The correction has to preserve the blade path and the interlock-pin relationship rather than forcing the handle closed by cosmetic trimming. |
| T6 | Reinstall the service disconnect in one continuous motion, confirm full blade entry, verify the latch hook snaps fully into the window, and check that the orange handle now remains flush with the cover plane instead of rebounding or sitting with a visible gap. |
| T7 | Restore the service lid, wake the pack, and confirm through two seat checks that the HVIL path, service-disconnect status, and ready-state permissive remain stable with no handle movement, no latch relaxation, and no repeat interlock warning. The release condition is a disconnect that seats flush and keeps the HVIL path stable after the lid and surrounding trim return to final position. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: At Velorian PackLine-287 AWD, reproduce the service-disconnect seating complaint by noting latch feel, insertion force, handle flushness, any HVIL warning, and whether the orange handle sits proud of the battery access opening or snaps back after seating. Keep the opening complaint tied to the actual handle position and HVIL response so later seat checks stay on the same disconnect boundary.

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Depower the pack, verify zero residual high-voltage potential at the service-disconnect cavity, and remove the access lid so the disconnect plug, blade set, guide rail, interlock pins, latch window, and surrounding sealing edge are all visible in one service view.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Pull the service disconnect straight out without rotating it, then inspect the male blades, female socket faces, guide ribs, CPA feature, handle pivot, and latch hook for burrs, arc marks, plastic rollover, uneven insertion witness lines, or debris in the guide channel.

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Compare the suspect disconnect with a known-good reference or spare, checking seating depth, latch travel, blade exposure, guide-rib alignment, and the shoulder position of the interlock pins so the seat issue is tied to geometry rather than to feel alone.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Rework the seat condition by correcting the misaligned guide feature, removing only the minor burr or displaced plastic that blocks full travel, and cleaning the contact area without changing the original blade profile, terminal tension, or latch geometry. The correction has to preserve the blade path and the interlock-pin relationship rather than forcing the handle closed by cosmetic trimming.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Reinstall the service disconnect in one continuous motion, confirm full blade entry, verify the latch hook snaps fully into the window, and check that the orange handle now remains flush with the cover plane instead of rebounding or sitting with a visible gap.

concept_mentions.label: handle flush with cover plane
concept_mentions.description: state of handle flush with cover plane

concept_mentions.label: latch hook fully snapped
concept_mentions.description: state of latch hook fully snapped

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: Restore the service lid, wake the pack, and confirm through two seat checks that the HVIL path, service-disconnect status, and ready-state permissive remain stable with no handle movement, no latch relaxation, and no repeat interlock warning. The release condition is a disconnect that seats flush and keeps the HVIL path stable after the lid and surrounding trim return to final position.

evidence_records.raw_text:
| Time step | O&M sample text |
|---|---|
| T1 | Start the HV output corrosion review on Helion E-Motion-536 Plus by noting whether corrosion was found during scheduled service or after a moisture complaint, and capture the as-found condition of the HV output boot, stud cover, cable tray, and surrounding pack wall before any hardware is loosened. |
| T2 | Isolate the pack, remove the HV output stud cover, and expose the copper cable lug, terminal nut, washer stack, plated stud thread, insulating boot, and stud shoulder without changing the cable angle more than necessary. Keep the cable angle and boot clocking readable so the later moisture-path check is tied to the true as-found geometry. |
| T3 | Inspect the corrosion pattern across the terminal nut flats, lug face, stud thread, washer stack, boot interior, and nearby insulator surface to determine whether the source is trapped water, road contamination, galvanic attack, or reduced clamp load. This pattern review should separate clamp-loss corrosion at the contact stack from moisture trapped under the boot or runoff entering from outside the pocket. |
| T4 | Remove the nut and washers in sequence, lay the hardware out by order, and examine the underside of the cable lug, the stud seating land, and the first thread turns for pitting, oxide buildup, contact shadowing, or heat tint beneath the contact face. |
| T5 | Measure contact resistance from the cable lug to the stud and compare it with the opposite-side output connection, taking care that the probe points land on clean metal and not on corroded thread, coated washer faces, or discolored nut surfaces. |
| T6 | Inspect the rubber boot lip, the drain path around the cover edge, the cable-entry orientation, and the nearby harness support to see whether water tracked down the cable, bypassed the boot seal, or sat trapped at the bottom of the stud pocket. |
| T7 | If the joint is under inspection only, reassemble the washer stack in the documented order and restore the insulating boot with the cable held at its natural exit angle. If pitting, thread damage, or heat history exceeds reuse limits, tag the exact stud, lug, washer, or nut for replacement and name that failed hardware explicitly in the record. |
| T8 | Close the stud pocket and restore the cover only after the record includes the corrosion location, corrosion severity, hardware order, moisture path assessment, and measured resistance comparison to the healthy reference output joint. The release note should still let the next technician identify the exact stud stack, corrosion source, and moisture path without reopening the pocket to rediscover the boundary. |

task.label: T1
task.surface_form: Start the HV output corrosion review on Helion E-Motion-536 Plus by noting whether corrosion was found during scheduled service or after a moisture complaint, and capture the as-found condition of the HV output boot, stud cover, cable tray, and surrounding pack wall before any hardware is loosened.

task.label: T2
task.surface_form: Isolate the pack, remove the HV output stud cover, and expose the copper cable lug, terminal nut, washer stack, plated stud thread, insulating boot, and stud shoulder without changing the cable angle more than necessary. Keep the cable angle and boot clocking readable so the later moisture-path check is tied to the true as-found geometry.

task.label: T3
task.surface_form: Inspect the corrosion pattern across the terminal nut flats, lug face, stud thread, washer stack, boot interior, and nearby insulator surface to determine whether the source is trapped water, road contamination, galvanic attack, or reduced clamp load. This pattern review should separate clamp-loss corrosion at the contact stack from moisture trapped under the boot or runoff entering from outside the pocket.

task.label: T4
task.surface_form: Remove the nut and washers in sequence, lay the hardware out by order, and examine the underside of the cable lug, the stud seating land, and the first thread turns for pitting, oxide buildup, contact shadowing, or heat tint beneath the contact face.

task.label: T5
task.surface_form: Measure contact resistance from the cable lug to the stud and compare it with the opposite-side output connection, taking care that the probe points land on clean metal and not on corroded thread, coated washer faces, or discolored nut surfaces.

task.label: T6
task.surface_form: Inspect the rubber boot lip, the drain path around the cover edge, the cable-entry orientation, and the nearby harness support to see whether water tracked down the cable, bypassed the boot seal, or sat trapped at the bottom of the stud pocket.

task.label: T7
task.surface_form: If the joint is under inspection only, reassemble the washer stack in the documented order and restore the insulating boot with the cable held at its natural exit angle. If pitting, thread damage, or heat history exceeds reuse limits, tag the exact stud, lug, washer, or nut for replacement and name that failed hardware explicitly in the record.

task.label: T8
task.surface_form: Close the stud pocket and restore the cover only after the record includes the corrosion location, corrosion severity, hardware order, moisture path assessment, and measured resistance comparison to the healthy reference output joint. The release note should still let the next technician identify the exact stud stack, corrosion source, and moisture path without reopening the pocket to rediscover the boundary.
